questions.question: In a conventionally cast ingot, where is chemical segregation of carbon, sulfur, and phosphorus most severe, and why?
options: At the mold walls, because they freeze first
In the upper center, because those elements concentrate in the last liquid to freeze
Uniformly throughout, because the liquid is stirred by convection
At the bottom, because the elements are heavier than iron
questions.explanation: Slow-freezing solute elements are rejected ahead of the solidification front and end up enriched in the last region to freeze - the upper center - which is why the crop also removes the worst segregation.

questions.question: A sand casting radiograph shows numerous smooth, round, well-defined dark spots scattered through one region. The most likely discontinuity and cause are:
options: Hot tears from a rigid core
Sand inclusions from gate erosion
Gas porosity from mold or core moisture and poor venting
Microshrinkage from a starved riser
questions.explanation: Smooth round dark spots are the classic image of gas porosity; moisture in the sand or cores and blocked vents generate the gas that is trapped as the metal freezes.

questions.question: What is a cold shut in a casting, and how does it typically image on film?
options: A crack from quenching - a ragged branched line
Two converging metal streams that were too cold to fuse - a smooth, continuous dark line with rounded ends
Trapped ceramic shell - an irregular light patch
A chilled surface zone - a uniform light band
questions.explanation: When two streams meet after cooling too far they fail to fuse, leaving a smooth linear discontinuity whose rounded ends and regular path distinguish it from the ragged, branched look of a tear or crack.

questions.question: In true centrifugal casting of pipe, where do slag, dross, and gas concentrate, and how is that region dealt with?
options: At the outside diameter, where they are ground off
At the bore (inside diameter), where the lighter constituents are driven by centrifugal force and removed by machining
At mid-wall, where they must be weld-repaired
At the pipe ends, which are cropped
questions.explanation: Centrifugal force packs the dense metal against the mold wall and drives the light nonmetallics and gas inward; the unsound bore layer is machined away, leaving a dense outer wall.

questions.question: As a general rule, a tight planar discontinuity is most detectable by radiography when:
options: Its plane lies parallel to the film
It is located at mid-thickness
Its plane lies along the beam direction, presenting the greatest change in metal path
The technique uses the lowest possible kilovoltage
questions.explanation: RT records changes in transmitted intensity, so a planar flaw images best when the beam travels along its plane; perpendicular to the beam it removes almost no metal path and may not image at all.

questions.question: Internal bursts in a forging are caused by:
options: Working the metal too cold or taking too large a reduction, rupturing the center of the section
Hydrogen absorbed from damp dies
Excessive grain refinement from too many blows
Slag from the original weld repair
questions.explanation: When forging temperature is too low or the bite too aggressive, the interior cannot flow with the surface and tears open at the center - ruptures that ultrasonic testing normally finds.

questions.question: Near the trailing end of an extrusion, an annular (ring-like) dark indication appears on the radiograph. The most likely cause is:
options: Chevron cracking from die chatter
The back-end defect - oxidized billet skin that flowed into the center at the end of the ram stroke
A lamination from the original slab
Fir-tree cracking on the surface
questions.explanation: At the end of the push the oxidized billet surface flows inward and rides into the product as an oxide-lined annular discontinuity; mills crop the back end to remove it.

questions.question: Two parallel dark lines run along the groove faces of a multi-pass stick weld. This 'wagon track' pattern is:
options: Slag wedged at the toes of a pass along the bevels and not removed before the next pass
Tungsten spatter from the electrode
Underbead hydrogen cracking
A film handling artifact
questions.explanation: Wagon tracks are elongated slag inclusions left in the grooves beside a bead - a signature SMAW interpass-cleaning failure that images as twin dark lines following the groove faces.

questions.question: Short light lines crossing the root image inside the bore of a pipe weld made with a wire-fed gun are most likely:
options: Cracks opened by root shrinkage
Burn-through cavities
Whiskers - electrode wire stubs that poked through the root and add metal to the beam path
Elongated porosity (hollow bead)
questions.explanation: In GMAW pipe roots, wire can stab through the puddle and remain as stubs in the bore; the extra metal absorbs more radiation, so whiskers image light, unlike voids, which image dark.

questions.question: Which statement about electroslag welding (ESW) is correct?
options: It is a low-heat-input process used for thin sheet
It maintains an arc throughout the weld under granular flux
It requires a vacuum chamber
It fills very thick vertical joints in a single pass by resistance heating of a molten slag bath, with coarse grain and centerline cracking as known risks
questions.explanation: Once running, ESW has no arc - current resistance-heats the conductive slag pool between water-cooled shoes - and its huge heat input and slow solidification bring coarse grain, centerline segregation and cracking, and restart defects.
